title: What drives euro area consumers to Chinese e-commerce platforms
gist: An April 2026 ECB Consumer Expectations Survey finds Chinese e-commerce platforms (Temu, Shein, AliExpress) are used by more than half of euro area consumers, drawn overwhelmingly by low prices and product variety. Geopolitics barely deters demand, and the flood of cheap imports may be helping suppress euro area consumer goods inflation.

insights.claim: Shipments of low-value items (up to EUR 150) from China into the EU more than doubled from 1.9 billion items in 2023 to 4.17 billion items in 2024, with China accounting for around 90% of all such e-commerce shipments in 2024.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: The doubling of low-value Chinese parcels signals a structural import channel that pressures European retailers and complicates customs, tariff and de minimis policy debates.
insights.quote: in 2024 around 90% of all e-commerce shipments entering the EU with a value of up to €150 came from China
insights.locator: Chinese e-commerce platforms are widely used across the euro area

insights.claim: More than half of ECB Consumer Expectations Survey respondents reported having purchased from Chinese e-commerce platforms such as Temu, AliExpress and Shein.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: Majority adoption shows these platforms are now mainstream European retail infrastructure, not a fringe channel, raising the competitive stakes for incumbents.
insights.quote: More than half of the CES respondents reported having used Chinese e-commerce platforms.
insights.locator: More than half of the CES respondents

insights.claim: Usage is highest in southern Europe, exceeding 70% of consumers in Greece, Portugal and Spain, while remaining below 50% in Germany and France.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: The north-south split flags where local retailers face the sharpest cheap-import competition and points to differing delivery infrastructure, trust and retail alternatives across the bloc.
insights.quote: The popularity of Chinese platforms is highest in Greece, Portugal and Spain, where more than 70% of consumers reported shopping on these platforms.
insights.locator: The popularity of Chinese platforms is highest

insights.claim: Use of Chinese platforms is more common among lower and middle-income households, consistent with the strong role of affordability, though many higher-income households also shop on them.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: Budget-constrained households are the core demand base, meaning cost-of-living pressures directly feed platform growth and any income squeeze accelerates it.
insights.quote: use is more common among lower and middle-income households
insights.locator: Indeed, the survey shows that use is more common

insights.claim: Price considerations dominate open-text responses, with words like price, cheap and cheaper appearing most frequently as the main reason frequent buyers give for using the platforms.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: Because demand is anchored to price rather than brand loyalty, these platforms have a durable structural advantage that is hard for higher-cost European rivals to erode.
insights.quote: Words such as “price”, “cheap” and “cheaper” come up most often in survey responses
insights.locator: Low prices and variety shape shopping decisions

insights.claim: Product variety is the second major driver, with consumers valuing niche items, accessories and goods difficult to find in local shops.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: The long-tail assortment advantage means these platforms compete on selection as well as price, widening the moat against traditional retail.
insights.quote: consumers value the broad range of products on offer, including niche items, accessories and goods that may be difficult to find in local shops
insights.locator: However, a second major driver is product variety

insights.claim: Consumers continue to hunt for cheaper alternatives even though euro area inflation has returned to a moderate, stable level since its 2020-21 peak.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: Bargain-seeking behaviour has become sticky and outlasts the inflation shock, suggesting demand for cheap imports persists even as price pressures ease.
insights.quote: consumers still appear to compare prices closely and seek cheaper alternatives
insights.locator: Inflation in the euro area has returned to a moderate level

insights.claim: Consumers most often buy clothing, household goods and electronics from Chinese platforms, categories where shoppers are especially price-sensitive.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: Concentration in these categories identifies exactly which European retail segments face the most intense cheap-import pressure.
insights.locator: What do consumers buy and how often?

insights.claim: Almost two-thirds of orders are worth no more than EUR 25 and 90% are up to EUR 50, while one in five respondents shop on these platforms at least once a month.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: High-frequency, low-ticket purchasing reflects an entrenched habitual behaviour that is resistant to displacement by sentiment shifts.
insights.quote: almost two-thirds are worth no more than €25 and 90% are up to €50
insights.locator: The vast majority of orders are of small value

insights.claim: For many of the goods categories most frequently bought on these platforms, average HICP inflation over 2025 was negative or close to zero, consistent with cheap imports helping keep a lid on consumer prices.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: This is the central macro signal for investors: low-cost Chinese imports may be a disinflationary force in euro area goods, relevant for ECB policy and rate expectations.
insights.quote: average inflation over 2025 was negative or close to zero
insights.locator: In fact, for many of the categories of goods

insights.claim: Almost half of respondents do not use Chinese platforms, deterred mainly by concerns over low perceived product quality, trust and environmental impact.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: Quality and trust deficits are the main brake on further penetration, so improvements there could unlock the remaining half of the market and intensify retailer competition.
insights.quote: Non-users indicated being put off by concerns about low perceived product quality, trust, and environmental impact

insights.claim: Non-users cited concerns about personal data security, payment safety, customer service and platform reliability, especially among those with limited cross-border online shopping experience.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: Data and payment-security worries mark a specific friction point that regulation or platform investment could address to expand adoption.
insights.quote: Respondents also mentioned concerns about personal data security, payment safety, customer service and the reliability of platforms.
insights.locator: Respondents also mentioned concerns about personal data security

insights.claim: Some non-users expressed a preference for buying European products to support local businesses, protect European jobs, and favour goods produced under higher regulatory, labour or environmental standards.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: A values-driven segment offers European retailers a defensible positioning around provenance, standards and sustainability rather than price.
insights.quote: non-users also frequently expressed a preference for buying European products, and a desire to support local or regional businesses and protect European jobs
insights.locator: Finally, non-users also frequently expressed a preference

insights.claim: Only one in five respondents said geopolitical concerns made them less inclined to use Chinese platforms, and most did not connect such concerns to their shopping decisions.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: Contrary to a common assumption, geopolitical tension is a weak lever on consumer behaviour, so trade rhetoric alone is unlikely to curb Chinese platform demand.
insights.quote: While one in five respondents indicated that geopolitical concerns made them less inclined to use such platforms, most did not appear to connect such concerns directly to their shopping.
insights.locator: Geopolitics do not seem to deter many consumers

insights.claim: Demand for low-cost Chinese goods is embedded in euro area consumer behaviour and unlikely to be easily displaced by shifts in sentiment alone.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: Structural, sentiment-resistant demand means the disinflationary import channel and competitive pressure on European retailers are likely to persist.
insights.quote: The demand is unlikely to be easily displaced by shifts in sentiment alone.

insights.claim: If Chinese platforms become even more prevalent, the current quality, trust and environmental concerns holding back half of consumers could give way to greater competitive pressure on European retailers.
insights.speaker: European Central Bank
insights.so_what: The forward risk for European retail earnings is a widening of platform penetration once trust barriers erode, worth monitoring for margin and pricing power impacts.
insights.quote: if such platforms become even more prevalent, this pattern could translate into greater competitive pressures for European retailers
insights.locator: Quality concerns, trust issues and environmental considerations
